sanskrit_devanagari:
न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत् ।
कार्यते ह्यवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः ॥ ५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na hi kaścit kṣaṇam api jātu tiṣṭhaty akarma-kṛt |
kāryate hy avaśaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛti-jair guṇaiḥ || 5 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Everyone is forced to act helplessly according to the qualities he has acquired from the modes of material nature; therefore no one can refrain from doing something, not even for a moment.
Everyone is forced to act helplessly according to the qualities he has acquired from the modes of material nature; therefore no one can refrain from doing something, not even for a moment.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1572 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: It is not a question of embodied life, but it is the nature of the soul to be always active. Without the presence of the spirit soul, the material body cannot move. The body is only a dead vehicle to be worked by the spirit soul, which is always active and cannot stop even for a moment.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.5 Hi, because; na kascit, no one; jatu, ever; tisthati, remains; api, even; for so much time as a ksanam, moment; akarma-krt, without doing work. Why? Hi, for; sarvah, all creatures; karyate karma, are made to work; verily avasah, under compulsion; gunaih, by the gunas-sattva (goodness); rajas (ac…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading restricts the verse's scope: 'the word unenlightened has to be added to the sentence, since the men of realization have been spoken of separately.' The unenlightened are made to act helplessly (avaśaḥ) by the guṇas; the man of jñāna-niṣṭhā is treated apart. The verse's force, on this reading: for the actor still in saṁsāra, action-cessation is not possible; therefore karma-yoga (action with purification of mind) is the only available path until jñāna-niṣṭhā is attained.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.5 In this world, no man can rest without doing work; for every person, even though he may have determined, ‘I will not do anything,’ is caused to act, i.e., is compelled to act according to the Gunas born of Prakrti.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the verse is a structural claim about Prakṛti's compulsion: the guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) increase in accordance with one's old karma; the actor is helplessly driven. jñāna-yoga can be attained only after the inner organ is purified by annulling old accumulation through karma-yoga. The verse's force: karma-yoga is the means by which the very compulsion the verse names is broken.

positions.rendering: forced to act helplessly / according to the qualities he has acquired
compelled to act by their qualities born of material nature
avaśaḥ = under compulsion; the unenlightened are made to work by the gunas; the verse applies to the unenlightened (men of realization treated separately)
compelled to act according to the gunas born of Prakrti, which increase in accordance with one's old karma; jnana yoga only attainable after annulling old accumulation through karma yoga

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The disagreement is about the SCOPE of the claim. The Advaita reading explicitly limits 3.5 to the unenlightened: the man of realization (sthita-prajña, jñāna-niṣṭha) is treated separately and is NOT compelled by the guṇas in the same sense. The Vishishtadvaita reading extends the claim further into Prakṛti's compulsion via accumulated karma; karma-yoga is the means to break the compulsion. Modern translators leave the scope unstated, which can read as a universal claim about all beings forever — sharper than either ācārya intended.

literal_meaning: Indeed, no one ever stays even for a moment without performing action; everyone is helplessly compelled to act by the guṇas born of prakṛti.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.5 is the metaphysical claim under 3.4. na hi kaścit kṣaṇam api jātu tiṣṭhaty akarma-kṛt — no one EVER stays even a moment without performing action. Why? kāryate hy avaśaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛti-jair guṇaiḥ — everyone is HELPLESSLY (avaśaḥ) made to act by the guṇas born of prakṛti. Per the Advaita reading: this applies to the unenlightened; the man of realization is treated separately. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: until the old accumulation of karma is annulled, the guṇas drive the actor. The verse closes off the fantasy of action-cessation. Action is not a choice that can be declined; the question is only the kind of action and the orientation of the actor.
